Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd try to set up a schedule at the start of each week, but with limited resources and needing to work part-time, things rarely went according to plan. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating diagrams and mind maps from the materials, which took longer than just reading. Most of my serious studying happened late at night when I finally had quiet time, often in intense bursts rather than steady sessions.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I could barely study at all because of work or family obligations, and then I'd have to cram everything into a few days. When exams approached, I'd lock myself in and study intensively for hours. The 21 hours I put in weekly weren't spread evenly—some weeks were maybe 5 hours, others were 40.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new announcements or when an assignment deadline was coming up that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch lecture videos when I had a decent internet connection. Fear of missing something important was a big motivator for me—I was always scared I'd miss a deadline or an update that could hurt my grade.
Q4: For a while, I got into the habit of logging in every morning with my coffee to check if anything new was posted. It became routine during the middle of the semester when things were more predictable. But when stress got really high or life got complicated, that routine fell apart quickly. I'd go days without checking, then panic and log in multiple times trying to catch up.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me—being a visual learner, I needed to see concepts explained, not just read about them. I watched those videos over and over, pausing and rewinding constantly. The peer interaction features were also helpful because I could see how other students approached problems, which gave me different perspectives. Discussion posts, even though I didn't write many, helped me feel less alone in struggling with difficult concepts.
Q6: I often ignored the discussion forums for posting my own thoughts—I'd read them but felt too anxious to contribute much. Some of the supplementary readings I skipped entirely because I didn't have time and had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades. The optional practice quizzes I mostly avoided because they felt like extra pressure when I was already overwhelmed with the required work.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to exam content or assignments. Visual materials, interactive elements, anything that helped concepts click in my head—those were valuable to me. If something felt like busywork or I couldn't see the payoff, I'd skip it because my time and energy were too limited to waste on things that wouldn't help my grade.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones created this pressure that I had to manage carefully, but at least I knew they mattered. With optional stuff, there was always this internal debate about whether the benefit was worth the time investment when I was already stretched thin. Sometimes I felt guilty for skipping optional materials, but survival mode meant focusing on essentials.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded lecture slides and videos to study offline because my internet isn't always reliable and I couldn't afford to stream everything repeatedly. I'd print out diagrams and annotate them by hand, create my own visual summaries on paper. A lot of my learning happened with textbooks from the library and YouTube videos that explained concepts differently than the course materials.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours studying from printed materials or my handwritten notes without touching the LMS at all. I'd work through problems on paper, draw concept maps on my wall, or study with a classmate in person. Those intense offline study sessions before exams were some of my most productive learning times, but they left zero trace in the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video once, then watch it five or six more times offline over the following week. Same with PDFs—I'd access them once to download, then study from the saved copy on my laptop for days. My limited data plan meant I had to be strategic about what I accessed online versus what I could study offline.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. I was dealing with high stress, feeling scared about whether I could actually pull this off, and my motivation was tanking. There were weeks where just opening the LMS felt overwhelming because I knew how behind I was. Family financial pressures and work demands piled up, making it hard to focus on academics even when I desperately wanted to.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely for a while. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between attending class, working, or studying—and studying from the LMS often lost that battle.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have videos playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about bills or other problems. I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but nothing was actually sinking in. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than genuine learning—my body was present but my brain was checked out.
Q15: I avoided assignments that felt too difficult or that I didn't understand, even when I knew they were important. The fear of failing or doing poorly made it easier to just... not start. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I understood better, but tomorrow kept getting pushed back. It was a self-protective thing, but it hurt my assignment completion rate badly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS usage. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly log in frantically, watching videos at 2x speed and downloading everything I could. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged much at all some weeks. They created panic, but also the pressure I needed to actually get things done.
Q17: Feedback was huge for me when I actually got it. Positive feedback on an exam gave me hope and motivated me to keep pushing despite everything. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made me spiral a bit, especially when I was already stressed. The CBT sessions I had helped me process feedback more constructively instead of catastrophizing about it.
Q18: The course structure was a double-edged sword. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me know what to prioritize, but the pacing sometimes felt relentless when life got complicated. The flexibility of online courses was essential for me given my situation, but it also meant I could fall behind easily without anyone noticing until it was too late.
Q19: The visual elements in the course materials made engagement easier for me—diagrams, video demonstrations, anything I could see rather than just read. What made it harder was when materials were text-heavy or when there were too many small tasks to track. The lack of flexibility on some deadlines was tough when I was dealing with real-life crises.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my bad days—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, clicking around trying to understand what's needed, but being too overwhelmed or confused to actually complete the work. High activity might show someone who's struggling and seeking help rather than someone who's thriving. Fear and confusion can drive a lot of clicking without producing submissions.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My exam score was really high even though my attendance and assignment completion were low because I studied intensively offline. I learned the material deeply through my own methods—visual notes, downloaded videos watched repeatedly, external resources. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual engagement with the content.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, timing around deadlines, and maybe consistency of engagement. But they completely miss the quality of learning happening offline, the mental state of the student while engaging, and whether clicks translate to actual understanding. They can't capture fear, determination, or the hours spent with printed notes away from a screen.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense to me, not just checking boxes. It's about caring enough to keep trying even when I'm scared and stressed, finding ways to learn that work for my brain. True engagement for me involves making connections between concepts, not just consuming content. It's an internal commitment that doesn't always show up in external metrics.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to succeed despite limited resources and high stress. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with hand-drawn diagrams on my wall, or studying from library books because I couldn't afford my own. It can't show the fear I felt constantly about failing, or the resilience it took to keep going when everything felt impossible. My distinction grade came from determination that no log could ever measure.
